role_summary: Boston Celtics center 1956-1969; 11-time NBA champion; first Black head coach in NBA history (1966); civil rights advocate.

photo.alt: Bill Russell dribbling, c. 1960, in his first years with the Boston Celtics
photo.license: Public domain (United States, pre-1978 publication without copyright notice)

biographical_paragraph: On the eve of the 1961-62 NBA season, Bill Russell and the Black members of the Boston Celtics were in Lexington, Kentucky for an exhibition game; a restaurant there refused to serve them, and rather than appear on the court the next day, the players who had been refused flew home [src: 2]. The action is remembered as the 1961 Celtics boycott [src: 2]. Russell was Monroe, Louisiana-born on February 12, 1934, played center for the Celtics from 1956 through 1969, won eleven championships and five Most Valuable Player awards in that stretch, and in 1966 became the NBA's first Black head coach, taking the bench while still active as a player [src: 2]. In 1967 he was among the African-American athletes who gathered in Cleveland to back Muhammad Ali's refusal of the draft, an event since called the Cleveland Summit [src: 2]. Russell died in Mercer Island, Washington on July 31, 2022, at eighty-eight [src: 2]; his oral history forms entry 8 of the Civil Rights History Project at the Library of Congress [src: 1].

sources.title: Bill Russell oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
